retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: For patients not tolerating oral weekly methotrexate a split oral dose of over 24 hours or weekly subcutaneous dose, and/or increasing the dose of  folic/folinic acid, is conditionally recommended over switching other DMARDs.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Patients treated with methotrexate should be prescribed folic acid supplements to reduce the adverse events associated with methotrexate therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Psoriatic Arthritis -- Treatment Planning -- Methotrexate
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with mild peripheral joint and skin disease Dose: 7.5 to 25 mg weekly by mouth or subcutaneously, usually given with 1 mg of folic acid daily Toxicities include oral ulcers, nausea, cytopenias, increased risk of infections, pulmonary toxicity (pneumonitis), and this drug is teratogenic Advise patients to avoid drinking excessive alcohol and be cautious with patients who have chronic kidney disease Check complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) every 2 to 4 months

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Advise patients for close follow-up.

retrieved_docs.title: Morphea -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who take methotrexate need a complete blood count and the comprehensive metabolic panel every three months to monitor for methotrexate toxicity, which can cause cytopenias and elevate liver function tests. Patients on methotrexate are prescribed folic acid supplements to prevent side effects like nausea, mucositis, and hair thinning due to folate depletion. Patients who are on long term corticosteroids should be screened for side effects like osteoporosis with dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of methotrexate toxicity, folinic acid (leucovorin) is used for rescue therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARD) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the difference between traditional and biological DMARDs. Identify the indications for various DMARD agents. Outline the adverse effects and contraindications to biologic DMARD therapy. Describe the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance care delivery for patients when using either traditional or biologic DMARDs. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initiation/titration of methotrexate to at least 15 mg as a weekly dose within 4 to 6 weeks is conditionally recommended over initiation/titration to <15 mg weekly dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: The typical interactions are with NSAIDs and PPIs due to the high frequency of concurrent prescriptions.

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: The coadministration of folic acid or its derivatives may reduce methotrexate's effectiveness in treating neoplastic diseases by competing for active transport across cell membranes. Patients should follow their healthcare provider's guidance regarding folic or folinic acid use to ensure optimal treatment outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrexate is a folic acid antagonist indicated for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis because of its high potency and efficacy; it can also help treat patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. [1] Gubner first suggested methotrexate as a treatment for rheumatoid arthritis after performing a double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial of methotrexate in patients with this condition. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Emphasis is placed on the role of interprofessional collaboration in managing methotrexate therapy. Understanding methotrexate's pharmacology supports the application of evidence-based medicine to optimize dosing, mitigate risks, and reduce adverse effects. This activity provides a foundation for healthcare professionals to deliver personalized and effective care, advancing therapeutic success and maintaining a high standard of care for patients receiving methotrexate therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Breastfeeding is generally contraindicated during maternal high-dose methotrexate therapy due to potential risks to the infant. Mothers should be advised to wait at least 1 week after receiving high-dose methotrexate before breastfeeding. To minimize potential exposure, withholding breastfeeding for 24 hours after a low-dose methotrexate administration may reduce the infant's exposure by approximately 40%. If breastfeeding is pursued with long-term low-dose methotrexate, monitoring the complete blood count and liver enzymes is recommended. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrexate (MTX) is an anti-metabolite commonly administered during chemotherapy and as an immunosuppressant for autoimmune diseases. This activity reviews the indications, mechanism of action, and contraindications for methotrexate as a treatment for neoplastic and autoimmune conditions. Adverse events, off-label uses, pharmacokinetics, administration methods, and key monitoring considerations are discussed to optimize patient outcomes. Relevant drug interactions and clinical toxicology are also highlighted to enhance safe and effective use.

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the mechanism of action of methotrexate in chemotherapy and autoimmune diseases. Identify the common and rare adverse events associated with methotrexate use. Assess the typical presentation of a patient who may be experiencing methotrexate toxicity. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to enhance patient care, ensure comprehensive methotrexate education, and optimize adherence, reducing morbidity and improving clinical outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: The use of oral methotrexate is conditionally recommended over the subcutaneous route for patients initiating methotrexate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Gold preparations are a class of therapeutic agents that contain gold and are used primarily as disease‑modifying antirheumatic drugs. Auranofin 3 mg oral capsule is an example of a gold preparation, classified as an auranofin formulation administered orally. Aurothiomalate sodium is also a member of the gold preparations class. Both auranofin and aurothiomalate sodium share the anti‑inflammatory properties of gold compounds and are employed in the management of rheumatoid arthritis and related conditions. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same gold preparation category helps clinicians anticipate similar efficacy and safety profiles, including potential adverse effects such as dermatitis, nephrotoxicity, and bone‑marrow suppression, and guides appropriate selection of gold‑based therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1283
retrieved_docs.content: Antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to treat or prevent anemia, and both folic acid and vitamin B12 are specific types of these preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: combined vitamin‑B formulations (e.g., folic acid 1 mg + vitamin B12 0.5 mg), and distinct vitamin B12 analogues (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin)—are organized within broader drug classes, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate supplementation and therapy regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: The oral tablet labeled “folic acid 1 MG / vitamin B12 0.5 MG” contains the two active ingredients vitamin B12 and folic acid, providing a combined supplement of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating a potential therapeutic role for this vitamin in managing the neurological disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 302
retrieved_docs.content: ascorbic acid (40 mg), ferrous fumarate (62.5 mg), folic acid (1 mg), niacin (3 mg), and polysaccharide iron complex (62.5 mg) in an oral capsule. In addition to iron‑based formulations, antianemic preparations also include agents based on folic acid and vitamin B12, reflecting the importance of addressing multiple micronutrient deficiencies in anemia management. Understanding this hierarchical classification and the detailed composition of each product helps clinicians select the most appropriate antianemic therapy tailored to individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1665
retrieved_docs.content: Auranofin 3 mg oral capsule is classified as a gold preparation, representing a member of the gold‑based drug class that is used therapeutically, particularly in the management of conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as agents containing vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic‑acid/vitamin B12 formulations. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin and analogues) is likewise placed within the broader category of vitamin B12 and folic‑acid compounds. Within this vitamin B12 group, individual forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are recognized as distinct members. Clinical assessment often includes laboratory measures that quantify both folic acid and cyanocobalamin (the active form of vitamin B12) in serum or whole‑blood specimens, providing a nominal‑type impression of the patient’s nutritional status. Together, these relationships clarify that antianemic therapy commonly relies on combined vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplementation, that various biochemical forms of vitamin B12 (including mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin) are part of this therapeutic class, and that combined measurement of folic acid and cyanocobalamin is a key tool for monitoring treatment effectiveness and guiding patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 328
retrieved_docs.content: dosage forms (chewable tablets, oral capsules, standard tablets, extended‑release tablets) to meet patient preferences and clinical needs. By delivering iron together with complementary vitamins and minerals, the products aim to prevent iron‑deficiency anemia, treat macrocytic (folate‑deficiency) anemia, support normal erythropoiesis and DNA synthesis, correct a wide range of post‑bariatric micronutrient deficits, and thereby enhance postoperative recovery and long‑term health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1521
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as formulations that contain vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic acid / vitamin B12 preparations. Clinical assessment of the effectiveness and adequacy of these anti‑anemic agents involves measuring vitamin B12 and folic acid levels, which are reported as “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型” – a laboratory notation indicating the impression, time point, and specimen type (serum or whole blood) for the nominal values of folic acid (叶酸) and cyanocobalamin (氰钴胺素, vitamin B12). This integrated understanding links the composition of anti‑anemic preparations with the standardized laboratory measures used to monitor patient response and guide therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: Acitretin, a systemic retinoid medication, is contraindicated in patients who have a liver disorder (اِضطرَاب الكَبِد), as the drug can cause hepatotoxic effects and exacerbate hepatic dysfunction; therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing acitretin to individuals with existing liver disease to ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: The Multivitamin Drug Class is linked to multivitamin preparations and encompasses specific formulations such as the folic acid 1 MG / vitamin B12 0.5 MG oral tablet, indicating that this tablet is an instance of the broader multivitamin drug category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: a component of several generic formulations described as “multivitamin with iron Multiple Vitamins with Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid, Iron and Docusate oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B12 with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” and “multivitamin with iron Vitamin C with Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet, extended release.” In addition to its nutritional role, folic acid is directly linked to the prevention and treatment of macrocytic anemia (فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات). Deficiency of folic acid impairs DNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells, leading to the production of abnormally large red blood cells and resulting in this type of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health
